narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 41% chance that you might not pass the bar exam on your first try, which is high enough to raise concerns about your long-term success. This prediction is based on key details from your profile, like your position in the bottom decile for Year 1 school grades, second-bottom decile for Year 3 grades, undergraduate GPA of 2.90, LSAT score of 31, and lower-middle family income bracket. Compared to typical applicants—who average mid-level deciles around 5 or 6, GPAs over 3.2, and LSATs near 37—your academic markers stand out as notably lower, placing you below most peers. The strongest factor pushing the model toward predicting bar failure was your Year 3 decile rank, as being near the bottom of your class there signaled potential challenges in sustaining performance. Right behind that, your Year 1 decile had a similar but slightly lesser pull toward failure, highlighting early struggles that didn't improve much. Your LSAT score was the next biggest influence in the same direction, well below the strong scores that usually predict bar success. Family income level and racial background as Asian each contributed moderately toward the failure prediction, though less than your academics, while your undergraduate GPA added a bit more weight in that direction too. Being male aligned slightly with higher risk in the model's view, but your choice to study full-time was the one feature that nudged things positively, toward a better chance of passing—though its impact was the smallest. Overall, your academic deciles and test scores dominated the decision, far outweighing demographics or study plans. To boost your future odds, focus on what you can control: retake the LSAT to aim for 35 or higher, build stronger study habits to climb those deciles in any future schooling, and seek resources like tutoring that match your full-time commitment. With targeted improvements in these areas, you could shift your profile closer to successful applicants and reapply stronger. Keep pushing—you've got the drive to turn this around.
